Q1: My study routine was honestly pretty chaotic. I tried to dedicate around 28 hours a week, but it wasn't organized in any structured way. I'm a hands-on learner, so I'd often spend time working through problems physically, writing things out, or trying to apply concepts practically rather than just reading. The anxiety I deal with made it hard to stick to a consistent schedule because some days I just couldn't focus at all.
Q2: My pattern varied a lot depending on what was due and how I was feeling mentally that day. Deadlines definitely pushed me to study more intensely, but I'd also have stretches where my anxiety would spike and I couldn't bring myself to engage at all. I tried to set regular times, but life and my mental state kept interfering with those plans. It was more reactive than proactive, honestly.
Q3: Usually it was an assignment deadline or needing to check if there were new announcements. Sometimes I'd log in because I felt guilty about not being active, even if I didn't end up doing much once I was there. The discussion posts were something I tried to keep up with, so that would prompt me to log in too. But I wasn't someone who logged in just to browse around.
Q4: Not really, if I'm being honest. I only logged in about twice a week regularly, and it never felt automatic or natural. The mindfulness exercises they introduced helped me manage some anxiety, but that didn't translate into more LMS usage. I think because I'm kinesthetic, I found more value in studying offline with physical materials than sitting at a screen.
Q5: The discussion forums were actually helpful because I could see how other students were thinking about problems. I posted quite a bit there—about 16 times—because talking through ideas helped me understand them better. The videos were okay too, I watched a decent amount, but I needed to pause and physically work through examples alongside them. Anything interactive felt more useful than passive reading.
Q6: I definitely ignored a lot of the reading materials and supplementary PDFs. They just didn't match how I learn—I need to do things with my hands, not just read about them. Some of the optional quizzes I skipped because my exam anxiety was already through the roof, and I didn't want more stress. I also delayed watching some video lectures because they felt like a passive activity.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could actually apply it to something concrete or if it helped me understand a concept I was struggling with. Discussion posts felt valuable because I was actively engaging, not just consuming. If something was just information dumping without any hands-on component, I'd lose interest quickly. I needed to feel like I was building something, not just memorizing.
Q8: Optional activities felt like a luxury I couldn't afford mentally. With my anxiety levels being what they were, I had to prioritize required work just to survive the course. Required assignments gave me structure and external pressure, which I actually needed to function. Optional stuff felt overwhelming because it was one more thing on the pile, even if it might have helped.
Q9: Absolutely, a lot of my studying happened offline. I'd download materials and work through them on paper, making diagrams and physically writing out solutions. I used external YouTube videos and other resources that explained things in more hands-on ways. My textbook got a lot more use than the LMS probably showed. This is where most of my 28 hours actually went.
Q10: There were many times I was deeply engaged with course material but the LMS would show nothing. I'd spend hours working through practice problems by hand at my desk, or I'd be discussing concepts with a friend over coffee. Sometimes I'd watch a video once, take detailed handwritten notes, and then study those notes for days without logging back in. The LMS only captured a fraction of my actual effort.
Q11: Yes, this was my standard approach. I'd access a lecture video or PDF once, download it or take notes, and then work with that material offline for extended periods. I found it easier to focus without being connected to the internet where distractions were everywhere. My anxiety also made it hard to be online for long periods, so offline studying was more comfortable for me.
Q12: There were definitely dark periods where engaging became almost impossible. When my anxiety peaked, I'd avoid the LMS entirely because just seeing my grades or pending assignments would trigger panic. My attendance dropped because I couldn't handle being in class some days. Around midterms, everything piled up and I felt paralyzed—surprised by how quickly things spiraled despite my efforts.
Q13: Fatigue and anxiety were the biggest factors affecting my LMS usage. When I was exhausted, I couldn't bring myself to log in and face what was waiting for me there. Competing responsibilities sometimes pulled me away, but honestly, it was more internal—the mental weight of knowing I was struggling made it harder to engage. Confusion with material also made me avoid certain sections entirely.
Q14: Yeah, there were times I'd open materials and just stare at them without anything sinking in. My mind would be racing with anxious thoughts, and I'd technically be 'studying' but not actually learning anything. I'd watch videos and realize at the end I hadn't absorbed a single thing. Those sessions felt like going through the motions without any real engagement.
Q15: I postponed exam preparation constantly because the thought of testing triggered intense anxiety. Even though I knew studying for exams was critical, I'd avoid it and focus on assignments instead because they felt more manageable. I also avoided asking for help when I was confused because I was embarrassed about struggling. That avoidance probably contributed to my low exam score.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me to act sometimes. I'd have bursts of activity right before something was due, cramming in work at the last minute. This wasn't healthy, but my anxiety made it hard to work on things early—I'd overthink and freeze. The deadline pressure actually cut through some of that paralysis and forced me to just do something.
Q17: Feedback had a complicated effect on me. Positive feedback would give me a temporary boost, but negative feedback could send me into an anxiety spiral where I'd avoid the course for days. I tried to use feedback constructively, but my low resilience meant setbacks hit me harder than they should have. Sometimes I'd delay checking feedback just to protect myself emotionally.
Q18: The course structure was honestly a bit overwhelming with 11 online courses to manage. Having so many modules made it hard to know where to focus my limited energy. The structure didn't really accommodate my kinesthetic learning style—it was mostly reading and watching. I needed more hands-on activities or practical applications built into the design.
Q19: The heavy reliance on videos and readings made engagement harder for me as a hands-on learner. What helped was when there were discussion components where I could actively participate. The mindfulness intervention they offered was surprisingly helpful for managing my anxiety, though it didn't directly help my grades. More interactive, practical elements would have made a huge difference.
Q20: I think that could happen when someone is anxious or confused and keeps checking materials without actually being able to complete work. They might be trying to understand but hitting walls repeatedly. Or maybe they're preparing in ways that don't translate to finished assignments—like me spending hours understanding concepts but then freezing when it came to exams. Activity doesn't equal productive output.
Q21: That actually resonates with me somewhat. I did a lot of studying offline that never showed up in the LMS, and I completed most of my assignments despite low login frequency. Someone could be efficient—downloading everything once and working independently. Or they might have strong prior knowledge. My issue was that despite offline effort, my exam anxiety destroyed my test performance.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns of when people access things and how often, which might indicate consistency or last-minute cramming. But they completely miss offline studying, emotional state, and the quality of engagement. They can't tell if someone is staring blankly at a screen versus deeply processing material. They also miss the anxiety and mental health factors that affect everything.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material and putting genuine effort into learning, not just completing tasks. It's about being mentally present and curious, wrestling with concepts until they make sense. For me, engagement often happened away from the computer—working through problems by hand, discussing ideas with peers, really trying to grasp things deeply.
Q24: The LMS would never show how much anxiety affected every aspect of my engagement. It wouldn't capture the hours I spent studying offline with handwritten notes and physical materials. It wouldn't show the mental battles I fought just to log in some days, or how the mindfulness exercises helped me cope. My effort and struggle are invisible in those numbers—only my failures show up clearly.
